response: The clinic must choose integer staffing levels by hour before uncertain walk-in arrivals occur, while satisfying shift-coverage constraints. More scheduled staff increase labor cost but provide greater service capacity, which can reduce the expected congestion and waiting costs generated when arrivals interact with fixed service rates. Fewer staff save wages but may produce longer queues and waits. The model therefore minimizes expected labor plus waiting cost using the arrival-rate forecast, wage and waiting-cost parameters, and fixed service rates. Staffing by hour is the managerial control; arrival counts, queue lengths, and waiting times are realized system states. Clinician speed is also not a control because service rates are fixed parameters. The waiting-cost weights represent managerial priorities as a model assumption. The supplied case does not establish that managers directly choose waiting time or can change service rates, so either interpretation would misstate the mechanism.
